locked_facts: Escape velocity: v_e = √(2GM/R) = √(2gR) — independent of mass of object
Orbital velocity (circular orbit at height h): v_o = √(GM/(R+h))
Near surface: v_o = √(gR) ≈ 7.9 km/s for Earth
v_e = √2 × v_o (at same radius) — escape velocity is √2 times orbital velocity
For Earth: v_e ≈ 11.2 km/s, v_o ≈ 7.9 km/s
Escape velocity is independent of the direction of projection
In orbit: gravitational force provides centripetal force — GMm/r² = mv²/r
truth_statements: Escape velocity depends only on the mass and radius of the planet, not on the mass of the escaping body
At any given radius the escape velocity is √2 times the orbital velocity
A satellite in circular orbit has negative total energy and its kinetic energy equals half the magnitude of its potential energy
Escape velocity is independent of the angle of projection — it depends only on the speed
For a satellite at height h above the surface orbital velocity decreases as v_o = √(GM/(R+h))

aha_moment.what_to_show: A projectile launched at different speeds: v < v_o falls back to Earth, v = v_o enters circular orbit, v_o < v < v_e enters elliptical orbit, v = v_e escapes on parabolic path. The √2 ratio between v_e and v_o highlighted.
aha_moment.visual_emphasis: Multiple trajectories from the same launch point — suborbital arc, circle, ellipse, parabola — with speed thresholds labelled
aha_moment.why_this_breaks_misconception: Students treat escape and orbital velocity as unrelated formulas — seeing them as speed thresholds on the same trajectory spectrum connects them

simulation_states.label: Orbital velocity
simulation_states.description: Satellite in circular orbit. Gravitational force = centripetal force gives v_o = √(GM/r). Higher orbit → slower speed. Energy bars: KE = ½|PE|.
simulation_states.emphasis: Gravity provides centripetal force — orbit is a balance between speed and gravity

simulation_states.label: Escape velocity
simulation_states.description: Object launched upward with v_e = √(2GM/R). KE bar exactly equals |PE| bar at surface. As object rises, both decrease. At infinity: KE = 0, PE = 0.
simulation_states.emphasis: At escape velocity, total energy = 0 — just barely reaches infinity

simulation_states.label: Speed thresholds
simulation_states.description: Four launches at increasing speeds. v < v_o: falls back. v = v_o: circular orbit. v_o < v < v_e: elliptical orbit. v = v_e: escapes. v_e = √2 × v_o shown.
simulation_states.emphasis: Same launch point, different speeds → fundamentally different trajectories

simulation_states.label: Interactive
simulation_states.description: Student adjusts launch speed and angle. Trajectory computed and drawn. Energy bars track KE, PE, total. Planet mass and radius adjustable to see how v_e and v_o change.
simulation_states.emphasis: Find the exact speed for circular orbit. Increase by √2 to escape.

common_misconceptions.belief: Escape velocity depends on the mass of the object
common_misconceptions.correction: Escape velocity v_e = √(2GM/R) does not contain the object's mass m — it cancels out in the energy equation. A feather and a rocket need the same speed to escape (ignoring air resistance).
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Launch objects of different masses at the same v_e — all escape identically

common_misconceptions.belief: A satellite stays in orbit because it is beyond Earth's gravity
common_misconceptions.correction: Gravity acts fully on the satellite — it provides the centripetal force for the circular orbit. The satellite is in free fall, continuously falling toward Earth but moving sideways fast enough to keep missing it.
common_misconceptions.simulation_emphasis: Show gravity arrow always pointing toward Earth centre — satellite is perpetually falling
